businessModel.text:
Goldman Sachs operates across four primary business segments:
  
    Investment Banking: Advisory (M&A, divestitures, restructurings) and equity/debt underwriting. Goldman is consistently ranked among the top three global M&A advisors and is particularly strong in high-profile, large-cap transactions. Fee revenue is highly cyclical and tied to global M&A and IPO activity levels.
    FICC and Equities Trading (Global Markets): Fixed-income, currencies, commodities (FICC), and equities trading for institutional clients. This is the highest-revenue segment in absolute terms and provides significant earnings stability through market cycles, though it is sensitive to volatility levels and client activity.
    Asset and Wealth Management: Traditional and alternative asset management (private equity, hedge funds, real estate, credit) alongside private wealth management for UHNW and HNW individuals. This segment has grown significantly and provides recurring fee revenue with some performance fee upside.
    Consumer & Wealth Management (Marcus / Personal Finance): The Marcus by Goldman Sachs digital banking platform and personal finance products. After the Apple Card partnership restructuring and exit from Marcus UK, the strategic scope of this segment is narrower than originally envisioned.
  
  Goldman's brand, talent density, and client relationships represent a durable competitive moat in investment banking and trading. The franchise's reputation for advising on the most consequential transactions globally is a self-reinforcing competitive advantage.

thesisEvaluation.text:
Bull Case
      75 / 100
      A sustained M&A and IPO recovery drives Investment Banking revenues significantly above current low baselines. The new CEO articulates a credible growth strategy that re-rates the shares. Marcus pivots to a profitable fintech partnership model without further capital destruction. Goldman's FICC and Equities franchise maintains market share. Shares re-rate to 20×+ earnings, driving 20–25 per cent total returns.
      Weight: 30%
    
    
      Base Case
      58 / 100
      M&A activity recovers modestly but not dramatically — a typical cyclical recovery rather than a structural rebound. Investment Banking contributes mid-single-digit revenue growth. Global Markets holds steady with mid-teens ROE. Asset management fees grow with AUM. EPS grows at 8–12 per cent annually. Shares drift to $950–1,050 range on 17–19× earnings.
      Weight: 50%
    
    
      Bear Case
      40 / 100
      M&A and ECM markets remain subdued as geopolitical uncertainty and rate volatility keep corporate boards cautious. Goldman's consumer finance write-offs continue or worsen. Global Markets revenues decline as reduced volatility and lower client activity compress trading volumes. The CEO transition disrupts strategic execution. Shares fall to $650–750 on 13–15× earnings.
      Weight: 20%

recommendation.text:
Conviction Score: 59/100 — REDUCE
  At 17.7× earnings and 7.8 per cent below the 52-week high, Goldman offers a reasonable valuation for the quality of the franchise but lacks the near-term catalysts to drive a re-rating. The M&A recovery is plausible but uncertain, and the CEO succession adds governance risk. Reduce exposure at current levels; add on weakness below $800.
  
    Buy Below$760
    HOLD Zone$760–$930
    Reduce Above$930
    Stop / Sell$700

entryExit.text:
GS — Goldman Sachs Group Inc (GS) — Entry / Exit Framework

Reduce / Trim: Above $925.96 — the stock is trading near or above the 52-week high. Capital should be rotated elsewhere.
Tighten or exit on any bounce above $998.58 — the risk/reward is poor at current levels.
Stop-loss (for remaining exposure): $1004.39 — a close above the 52-week high would confirm the bull case is breaking down.
Target: $532.11 — partial mean reversion toward the middle of the 52-week range.
